reasoning: The base rate for protests in Nauru is extremely low. Over the 360 days preceding the forecast date (Aug 17, 2025), ACLED recorded 0 protests, establishing the baseline to beat as 0.000. Resolving "YES" for any given date therefore requires at least 1 protest occurring in the 30-day window prior to that date. Historically, protests on Nauru are tied mainly to the offshore detention of asylum seekers. While there are currently around 90-100 transferees on the island and new controversial deportation laws were implemented in late 2024 (sparking legal challenges and tension), there is no evidence of an ongoing protest campaign. Because in-person public demonstrations of 3+ participants are required to qualify as an ACLED 'Protest', and these events happen generally once every few years, the average chance of a protest falling into any given 30-day window is very low (likely under 10%). For the first resolution date (Aug 24), the 30-day window ends in just 7 days, and no protests have been reported in the news over the past 3 weeks; thus, its probability is at the minimal floor. Predictions are staggered slightly higher for subsequent dates to reflect increasing forecast horizon uncertainty (regressing toward 0.5) and the ongoing unpredictable nature of the newly active refugee detentions.
